trajectory_summary: The call is a logistics transaction, not a setter call. The rep inherited a warm, cooperative customer and converted that warmth into a tentative booking without completing a single verify item, establishing legitimacy, anchoring bill pain, or creating curiosity. The appointment exists on the calendar but has no emotional foundation — the customer has no stated reason to show up beyond having agreed to a time. The outcome tag 'booked' is not wrong, but it is underspecified: this is a fragile booking, not a qualified sit, and the closer walks in with no context, no bill, and no verified qualification.
outcome_dispute: The v1 tag 'unknown' likely resolves to 'booked,' which is accurate as far as it goes. The more precise characterization is 'conditionally booked, fragile' — the appointment was set without any verify completion, without bill capture, and without co-owner identification. The tag 'booked' is an underspecification of the appointment's structural vulnerability, not an overstatement of it.

primary_coaching_focus.quote: So it looks like we have an evaluation for your home. Right? We just don't have an electric bill.
primary_coaching_focus.why: Brandon booked a sit on a customer with zero verify items completed. The manager cannot tell from this call whether Michael is a homeowner, who is on the title, what his bill pain is, or whether his roof qualifies. If this is a paid lead, the appointment may not count as a sit. The coaching conversation starts with one question: 'What did you know about Michael before you booked him?' — and then works forward from the answer to what Brandon should have gathered on the call.

key_moments.quote: My name is Brandon. I'm here with Solar Energy.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep introduces himself as being with 'Solar Energy' — not ION Solar, not the Tesla-certified installer framing the schema specifies. The intro beat's primary job is legitimacy: answering 'is this a scam?' in the first 30 seconds. 'Solar Energy' is a generic phrase that does the opposite — it sounds like every door-knocker and robocall the customer has ever received. Strategic hypothesis: the rep is deliberately softening the company name to reduce resistance. Reactive hypothesis: the rep simply failed to execute the intro. The reactive read holds — there is no evidence of a deliberate strategy, and the rest of the call shows no compensating legitimacy move.

The rep phrases the time anchor as a question with 'around' — 'Around 12:00 work for you?' This is a soft time anchor, not a locked one. The schema distinguishes callbacks with locked times from 'we'll touch base' arrangements on no-show rate. 'Around 12:00' sits closer to the latter. The customer confirms 'Monday at twelve' in the rep's follow-up restatement, which partially recovers the lock, but the rep's own phrasing introduced ambiguity first.

key_moments.quote: So it looks like we have an evaluation for your home. Right? We just don't have an electric bill.
key_moments.stewart_read: This is the rep's only qualification move in the entire call, and it is not a verify item — it is a bill-collection prompt. No address confirmation, no homeowner check, no title/co-owner question, no interest anchor, no utility company, no roof age. The verify section requires 6 of 8 items for a qualified appointment. Zero were completed. The rep is booking a sit on a customer whose qualification status is entirely unknown.

key_moments.quote: We just don't have an electric bill.
key_moments.stewart_read: The rep identifies the missing bill and defers collection to the appointment. The schema's bill_collection section distinguishes by lead type: paid leads require the bill before the appointment can be set; organic leads prefer it but do not require it. There is no evidence in the transcript that Brandon made a conscious lead-type determination. If this is a paid lead, deferring the bill is a protocol violation that puts the sit at risk. If this is an organic or recycled lead, deferral is permissible. The gap is not purely reactive behavior — it may reflect lead-type rule ignorance rather than willful avoidance — but the outcome is the same: the closer walks in without the bill.
